ucs.title: [LLM] Earth Lusca DLL side-loading via abused signed binaries staged outside Program Files
ucs.description: Hunts Earth Lusca's signature loader pattern: a legitimately-signed third-party binary (VLC, Notepad++ GUP, BitDefender AVKkid, Realtek RtkAudUService, Cobian Backup, VMware vmnat, etc.) executed from a staging directory (Temp, AppData, ProgramData, PerfLogs) so it side-loads an attacker-controlled DLL sitting next to it — the documented delivery for BIOPASS RAT, Cobalt Strike beacons and ShadowPad shipped by this group (Trend Micro, 2022; per Microsoft = Charcoal Typhoon / CHROMIUM, Recorded Future = TAG-22).
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where (Processes.process_name IN ("vlc.exe","GUP.exe","AVKkid.exe","RtkAudUService64.exe","Cobian.exe","vmnat.exe","cookie_exporter.exe","iusb3mon.exe","AppLaunch.exe","Util.exe") AND (Processes.process_path="*\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\AppData\\Roaming\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\ProgramData\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\Users\\Public\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\Windows\\Temp\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\PerfLogs\\*")) by host Processes.user Processes.process_name Processes.process_path Processes.parent_process_name Processes.process | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | convert ctime(firstTime) ctime(lastTime) | sort - lastTime
ucs.kql:
let LookbackDays = 7d;
// Signed binaries Earth Lusca / Charcoal Typhoon are documented to abuse for DLL side-loading
let _abused_loaders = dynamic(["vlc.exe","GUP.exe","AVKkid.exe","RtkAudUService64.exe","Cobian.exe","vmnat.exe","cookie_exporter.exe","iusb3mon.exe","AppLaunch.exe","Util.exe"]);
let _staging_paths = dynamic([@"\AppData\Local\Temp\", @"\AppData\Roaming\", @"\ProgramData\", @"\Users\Public\", @"\Windows\Temp\", @"\PerfLogs\"]);
DeviceImageLoadEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(LookbackDays)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ (_abused_loaders)
// loader binary is sitting in a staging dir, not Program Files
| where InitiatingProcessFolderPath has_any (_staging_paths)
// the loaded DLL lives in the SAME folder (classic side-load layout)
| where tolower(FolderPath) =~ tolower(InitiatingProcessFolderPath)
| where FileName endswith ".dll"
// loader and module are different files (i.e. it's actually loading something custom)
| where SHA256 != InitiatingProcessSHA256
| where InitiatingProcessAccountName !endswith "$"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName,
          LoaderBin = InitiatingProcessFileName,
          LoaderPath = InitiatingProcessFolderPath,
          LoaderSha256 = InitiatingProcessSHA256,
          LoadedDll = FileName,
          LoadedDllSha256 = SHA256,
          LoaderCmd = InitiatingProcessCommandLine,
          AccountName = InitiatingProcessAccountName
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.sentinel_kql:
let LookbackDays = 7d;
let _abused_loaders = dynamic(["vlc.exe","gup.exe","avkkid.exe","rtkaudUservice64.exe","cobian.exe","vmnat.exe","cookie_exporter.exe","iusb3mon.exe","applaunch.exe","util.exe"]);
let _staging_paths = dynamic([@"\AppData\Local\Temp\", @"\AppData\Roaming\", @"\ProgramData\", @"\Users\Public\", @"\Windows\Temp\", @"\PerfLogs\"]);
// Image-load events have no direct Sentinel equivalent — pivot on the loader being launched from a staging dir,
// which is the moment side-loading is set up. ImProcessCreate gives cross-vendor coverage (MDE/Sysmon/CrowdStrike).
ImProcessCreate
| where TimeGenerated > ago(LookbackDays)
| where ActorUsername !endswith "$"
| extend BinName = tolower(tostring(split(TargetProcessName, "\\")[-1]))
| where BinName in (_abused_loaders)
| where TargetProcessName has_any (_staging_paths)
| project TimeGenerated, DvcHostname, ActorUsername,
          LoaderPath = TargetProcessName,
          LoaderCmd = TargetProcessCommandLine,
          ParentProc = ParentProcessName,
          ParentCmd = ParentProcessCommandLine,
          SHA256
| order by TimeGenerated desc
ucs.rationale: Earth Lusca's published intrusions (Trend Micro Jan-2022 'Earth Lusca' report, Recorded Future TAG-22, Microsoft Charcoal Typhoon write-ups) repeatedly show the same delivery: a legitimately-signed third-party EXE is dropped into a user-writable staging directory together with a malicious DLL of a name the EXE will resolve via search order. Generic 'PowerShell from Word' detections will miss it; t

ucs.title: [LLM] Earth Lusca Exchange / IIS web-shell discovery: w3wp.exe spawning interactive recon binaries
ucs.description: Catches Earth Lusca's post-exploitation pattern after weaponising public-facing apps (ProxyLogon CVE-2021-26855, ProxyShell, Oracle WebLogic, Confluence). Once a web shell is dropped on Exchange/IIS, w3wp.exe spawns interactive shells for hands-on-keyboard recon (whoami / net group / nltest / systeminfo) — the documented Earth Lusca tradecraft from Trend Micro's 2022 report and Recorded Future's TAG-22 cluster.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdlines values(Processes.process_name) as children from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name="w3wp.exe" AND Processes.process_name IN ("powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","cmd.exe","cscript.exe","wscript.exe","bitsadmin.exe","certutil.exe","mshta.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe","whoami.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","ipconfig.exe","systeminfo.exe","tasklist.exe","quser.exe","nltest.exe","hostname.exe","wmic.exe","arp.exe","route.exe") by host Processes.user Processes.parent_process_name span=10m | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | where mvcount(children) >= 2 | convert ctime(firstTime) ctime(lastTime) | sort - lastTime
ucs.kql:
let LookbackDays = 7d;
// Earth Lusca recon set after Exchange / IIS web-shell — taken from Trend Micro IR observations
let _recon_children = dynamic(["powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","cmd.exe","cscript.exe","wscript.exe","bitsadmin.exe","certutil.exe","mshta.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe","whoami.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","ipconfig.exe","systeminfo.exe","tasklist.exe","quser.exe","nltest.exe","hostname.exe","wmic.exe","arp.exe","route.exe"]);
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(LookbackDays)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "w3wp.exe"
| where FileName in~ (_recon_children)
// classic IIS app-pool identities or system contexts running interactive recon
| extend AppPool = extract(@"-ap\s+\"?([^\"\s]+)\"?", 1, InitiatingProcessCommandLine)
| summarize ChildCount = count(),
            DistinctChildren = dcount(FileName),
            Children = make_set(FileName, 50),
            SampleCmds = make_set(ProcessCommandLine, 20),
            FirstSeen = min(Timestamp),
            LastSeen = max(Timestamp)
            by DeviceName, IISAccount = InitiatingProcessAccountName, AppPool, bin(Timestamp, 10m)
// >=2 distinct recon binaries from one w3wp inside 10 min == hands-on-keyboard, not a misconfigured plugin
| where DistinctChildren >= 2
| order by LastSeen desc
ucs.sentinel_kql:
let LookbackDays = 7d;
let _recon_children = dynamic(["powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","cmd.exe","cscript.exe","wscript.exe","bitsadmin.exe","certutil.exe","mshta.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe","whoami.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","ipconfig.exe","systeminfo.exe","tasklist.exe","quser.exe","nltest.exe","hostname.exe","wmic.exe","arp.exe","route.exe"]);
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated > ago(LookbackDays)
| where EventID == 4688
| where ParentProcessName endswith "\\w3wp.exe"
| extend ChildBin = tolower(tostring(split(NewProcessName, "\\")[-1]))
| where ChildBin in (_recon_children)
| summarize ChildCount = count(),
            DistinctChildren = dcount(ChildBin),
            Children = make_set(ChildBin, 50),
            SampleCmds = make_set(CommandLine, 20),
            FirstSeen = min(TimeGenerated),
            LastSeen = max(TimeGenerated)
            by Computer, Account, bin(TimeGenerated, 10m)
// >=2 distinct recon binaries spawned by one w3wp in 10 min == interactive operator, not benign plugin
| where DistinctChildren >= 2
| order by LastSeen desc
ucs.sigma_yaml:
title: Earth Lusca IIS Web Shell Recon via w3wp.exe Child Process
id: 4ad7e3a4-9f3e-4f7e-9d15-earthlusca-w3wp
status: experimental
description: Detects Earth Lusca / Charcoal Typhoon (G1006) post-exploitation of public-facing IIS / Exchange where a dropped web shell pivots to interactive recon. w3wp.exe is the IIS worker process and should never spawn cmd, powershell, or domain-recon LOLBins under normal application load.
references:
  - https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1006/
  - https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/22/a/earth-lusca-sophisticated-infrastructure-varied-tools-and-techni.html
author: SOC Detection Engineering
date: 2026/05/04
tags:
  - attack.initial_access
  - attack.t1190
  - attack.persistence
  - attack.t1505.003
  - attack.execution
  - attack.t1059.001
  - attack.t1059.003
  - attack.discovery
  - attack.t1057
  - attack.t1016
  - attack.t1482
  - earth_lusca
  - charcoal_typhoon
  - tag22
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection_parent:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\w3wp.exe'
  selection_child:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\powershell.exe'
      - '\pwsh.exe'
      - '\cmd.exe'
      - '\cscript.exe'
      - '\wscript.exe'
      - '\bitsadmin.exe'
      - '\certutil.exe'
      - '\mshta.exe'
      - '\rundll32.exe'
      - '\regsvr32.exe'
      - '\whoami.exe'
      - '\net.exe'
      - '\net1.exe'
      - '\ipconfig.exe'
      - '\systeminfo.exe'
      - '\tasklist.exe'
      - '\quser.exe'
      - '\nltest.exe'
      - '\hostname.exe'
      - '\wmic.exe'
      - '\arp.exe'
      - '\route.exe'
  condition: selection_parent and selection_child
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate IIS extensibility / monitoring agents that shell out (rare, document and exclude by app-pool name)
  - Sharepoint / Exchange admin-initiated diagnostics from EAC
level: high
ucs.rationale: Earth Lusca's intrusion lifecycle (per the Trend Micro 2022 publication and overlapping Recorded Future TAG-22 / Microsoft Charcoal Typhoon reporting) consistently begins with exploitation of internet-facing servers — ProxyLogon, ProxyShell, Oracle WebLogic, Atlassian Confluence — followed by a web shell such as the China Chopper variant they favour. The defining post-exploit fingerprint is w3wp.e
_sigma_issues: sigma parse error: Sigma rule identifier must be an UUID
